---
title: "AI-Assisted Trade Surveillance"
subtitle: "Data Science for Financial Services"
author: "Wu Jiayan"
date: "2026-05-12"
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
---

## Project overview

The lab examines how misconduct can be detected in trading-floor communications. It compares manual Excel keyword searches with an AI-assisted TradeGuard workflow built in Google AI Studio.

## Surveillance scope

The analysis searches for several forms of potential misconduct:

- Market manipulation, including spoofing, layering, and price fixing.
- Front-running ahead of a pending client order.
- Solicitation or disclosure of material non-public information.
- Codewords and non-approved languages used to conceal intent.

## Approaches compared

| Dimension | Excel keyword search | TradeGuard AI |
|---|---|---|
| Detection logic | Exact predefined terms | Terms plus contextual reasoning |
| Review effort | Manual review around every match | Automated segment analysis |
| False alarms | High when legitimate jargon matches | Better differentiation using intent |
| New patterns | Limited to known words | Can suggest emerging codewords |
| Regulatory context | Added manually | Can map behavior to regulatory concepts |

## Key learning: intent matters

Keyword matching is a useful transparent baseline, but misconduct is rarely captured by one word in isolation. The AI-assisted workflow linked conversation segments and interpreted intent, including:

- Differentiating legitimate market colour from suspicious instructions.
- Identifying possible concealed language such as newly emerging codewords.
- Linking confidential-information leakage with a subsequent trade.
- Explaining why a segment might represent spoofing or front-running.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Chat communications"] --> B["Dictionary and keyword screening"]
  B --> C["Context-window analysis"]
  C --> D["Intent and behavior classification"]
  D --> E["Regulatory mapping"]
  E --> F["Human compliance review"]
```

## Conclusion

Modern surveillance should move beyond static word hunting toward contextual intent analysis. AI can improve triage speed and surface linked behavior, but it should support rather than replace compliance professionals.

## Governance requirements

An operational implementation would require:

- A labelled evaluation set with precision, recall, and false-positive reporting.
- Human adjudication before any enforcement action.
- Explainable alerts with preserved source context.
- Access controls, privacy safeguards, and audit trails.
- Version-controlled surveillance dictionaries and prompts.
- Monitoring for model drift and language changes.

## Source artifact

- [Individual lab report](Individual%20Lab%20Report.docx)
